metadata.summary: Identify, score, and mitigate demand, competition, pricing, trend, and channel risks. Each risk row cites evidence from market research files, ensuring auditable decision gates.

content.title: Risk Assessment (Market-Researcher Lens)
content.summary: Market-risk assessment identifies, scores, and mitigates demand, competition, pricing, trend, and channel risks before committing to a segment or pricing strategy. Every risk row must cite a paragraph or table from an existing market research file — no row may rely on general knowledge. This ensures the register stays grounded in evidence and can be automatically validated.

why.p: Entrepreneurs either ignore risks until they materialize or paralyze themselves without structure. Standard H/M/L scoring collapses four distinct pricing failure modes into one row and anchors demand risk on TAM rather than the validated wedge. A citation-enforced register surfaces these hidden exposures and connects each High-priority row to a concrete trigger metric already in the company's dashboard.

list.item: Pre-entry go/no-go on a new market segment when TAM/SAM/SOM exists but demand evidence is thin
Pricing strategy decision: before locking a price point, score demand-elasticity and anchor-competitor risk
Launch-readiness review for a positioning or category bet
After a competitor raises a Series B or ships a copycat: re-score competitive-displacement and pricing-pressure rows
Channel-dependence audit when more than 40% of pipeline comes from one channel
Pivot evaluation: comparing two segment options requires a structured market-risk delta
Pure technical, team, financial, or operational risk — use the generic `researcher/risk-assessment` variant or `pro/pm/pm-traditional/risk-management/`
Idea stage with fewer than 5 problem interviews — demand-risk score is uncalibrated; run continuous discovery first
B2B deals where risk is per-account, not per-market — use account-level deal-risk frameworks
Solo side projects under $1k of committed spend — overhead exceeds expected loss

rule.statement: Every risk row must cite a paragraph or table from an existing market research file (market-research.md, competitive-analysis.md, pricing-research.md, trend-analysis.md, niche-evaluation.md). No row may cite general knowledge.
rule.rationale: Uncited risk rows are opinion, not analysis. Citation enforcement makes the register auditable, prevents stale scores, and forces re-run when source documents change.

section.name: risk-sub-categories
section.p: Five market-risk sub-categories with scoring rules:

list.item: Demand: market too small, poor timing, no validated wedge (note: score against the validated wedge, not the TAM)
Competition: incumbent copies feature, well-funded rival enters, adjacent player pivots in (include not-yet-entered threats)
Pricing: split into four sub-rows — commoditization, price war, anchor mismatch, willingness-to-pay collapse
Trend: technology obsolescence, regulatory shift, macro headwind reversing the adoption curve
Channel: single-channel dependence (auto High-prob when one channel exceeds 40% of pipeline)

rule.statement: Score probability and impact independently on H/M/L (3/2/1), multiply for Risk Score (1-9). Force at least 2 rows at H/H (score 9) and at least 2 rows at L/L (score 1) — reject balanced-looking registers where every row is M/M.
rule.rationale: Symmetric scoring (all Med/Med) is the most common agent bias; forcing distribution prevents false balance and surfaces real exposure.

list.item: High probability: more than 50% chance in the planning horizon
Medium probability: 20-50% chance
Low probability: below 20% chance
High impact: existential threat to the segment or pricing model
Medium impact: major setback requiring significant resource reallocation
Low impact: minor inconvenience, resolved within one sprint
Avoid: eliminate the risk entirely (high prob + high impact); typically means not entering a segment or not adopting a channel
Mitigate: reduce probability or impact (medium risks); requires concrete actions with named owners
Transfer: shift to insurance, contracts, or SLAs (low probability, high impact)
Accept: document and monitor (low risks)

rule.statement: Pair every High-priority row with exactly one of: kill-decision criterion, pivot criterion, or budget reallocation criterion. A risk row without a connected action is decoration.
rule.rationale: Risk registers that document threats without decision criteria are never acted on; the action criterion turns a log into a decision trigger.

rule.statement: Trigger metrics must be observable in the company's existing dashboard. "ARPU drops below $X for 2 consecutive months" works; "market sentiment shifts" does not.
rule.rationale: Unobservable triggers are never fired; the contingency plan is never activated; the risk register provides false security.

list.item: Step 1 — Enumerate risks: run the enumeration agent against market-research.md, competitive-analysis.md, pricing-research.md; require minimum 2 risks per sub-category (demand, competition, pricing, trend, channel)
Step 2 — Red-team pre-mortem: run a market-specific pre-mortem with 3 failure branches (segment did not materialize, competitor won, price elasticity broke the model)
Step 3 — Score with citations: apply prob x impact; every cell must cite a specific paragraph or table from the source files; reject rows without citations
Step 4 — Human review: owner accepts or rejects each row and assigns a trigger metric already visible in the company dashboard
Step 5 — Persist and schedule: save as market-risk-register.md; re-run enumeration and pre-mortem passes monthly or on any commit to source research files

rule.statement: Run the pre-mortem with 3 distinct agent personas (skeptical customer, well-funded competitor, regulator or platform owner) rather than a single agent pass. Consolidate outputs; do not average them.
rule.rationale: A solo agent run produces a single failure narrative that confirms the briefing document's tone. Multiple adversarial personas surface independent failure paths that the enumeration pass misses.

antipattern.description: Demand risk scored against TAM rather than the validated wedge
antipattern.reason: A large TAM with no validated entry wedge is the highest-loss failure mode; anchoring on TAM produces artificially low demand-risk scores.

antipattern.description: Pricing risk collapsed into one row instead of four sub-rows
antipattern.reason: Aggregating four distinct failure modes (commoditization, price war, anchor mismatch, WTP collapse) into one row averages them and hides the real exposure.

antipattern.description: Channel-dependence risk scored Medium when one channel exceeds 40% of pipeline
antipattern.reason: Any single channel above 40% should be auto-scored High probability unless 3+ years of stable retention evidence exists.

antipattern.description: Risk register updated only at project kick-off
antipattern.reason: Stale registers are worse than none; they create false security. Re-run at minimum monthly and on every commit to source research files.

antipattern.description: Agent quotes multi-billion-dollar TAM and scores demand risk Low
antipattern.reason: Force the prompt to score against the validated wedge (named buyer segment, confirmed problem, pipeline evidence), not the TAM ceiling.

antipattern.description: Agent uses stale competitor lists from training data
antipattern.reason: Require the agent to re-read competitive-analysis.md and verify competitor domains via faion-domain-checker-agent before citing any competitive risk row.

antipattern.description: Agent mitigation language defaults to "diversify channels", "monitor competitors", "iterate pricing"
antipattern.reason: Ban these phrases; require action verb + named owner + measurable trigger + budget cap. Vague mitigations are never executed.

antipattern.description: Agent ships "Avoid" response on market risk autonomously (e.g., killing a segment)
antipattern.reason: Avoid-strategy responses mean killing a segment, geo, or pricing tier — never let an agent execute this unattended. Hard human-in-the-loop gate required.

antipattern.description: Risk IDs change every agent run (R1..Rn renumbered)
antipattern.reason: Use a stable hash of the risk statement or instruct the agent to diff-merge against the existing register; changing IDs break longitudinal tracking.

template.purpose: Risk register template with high/medium/low priority tables and contingency plan format
Pre-mortem facilitation template with failure-mode consolidation and risk conversion table
Bash script that enforces citations from market-research files on every register row
